initialReview.label: Initial Review Date
initialReview.description:
On this date, DOCR and Parole Board staff review your case and set your Parole Review Date. Longer sentences may have multiple initial reviews before the Parole Review. **This is not the date the Parole Board decides whether you are granted parole.**

If you do not see a date here, it may be because the date has not been set yet, your sentence does not require one, or you may not be eligible for parole.

paroleReview.label: Parole Review Date
paroleReview.description:
This is when the Parole Board is scheduled to review your case for possible early release. The Board looks at your programs, conduct, and release plan, and can grant parole, defer your case, or deny parole. 

If the Board defers, you'll get a new Parole Review Date; if the Board grants parole, a parole date will be set (unlikely to be the same day as the hearing).

Not everyone has a Parole Review Date — it may not be set yet, or you may be ineligible for parole.

goodTime.label: Good Time Release Date
goodTime.description:
Your Good Time Release Date is calculated by subtracting 5 days from your maximum release date for every month of your sentence. You get this Good Time up front at the start of your sentence; losing Good Time through infractions will push this date later.

People serving life sentences do not have a Good Time Release Date.

EIGHTYFIVEPercent.label: 85% Date
EIGHTYFIVEPercent.description:
Some sentences require that 85% be served in a facility. If you have an 85% date, that is the earliest that you can be released, even if your Good Time Release Date shows an earlier date.

If no date is shown, the 85% rule does not apply to your sentence — it applies only to certain violent offenses.

finalSentExp.label: Maximum Release Date
finalSentExp.description:
The latest date you can be held in custody. Your sentence is legally complete on this date.

People serving life sentences do not have a Maximum Release Date.

sentenceDates.heading: Learn More About Your Sentence
sentenceDates.body:
## What do my dates mean?

Your sentence information shows several important dates. Some are *review dates* (when staff or the Parole Board look at your case). Others are *release dates* (when you might actually go home). Here's what each one means.

###  Review dates

*Initial Review Date.* On this date, DOCR and Parole Board staff set your Parole Review Date. This generally only occurs if your sentence is greater than 3 years. This is *not* the date the Parole Board hears your case.

*Initial Review Reassessment Date.*
On this date, DOCR and Parole Board staff check if your Parole Review Date needs to be moved. There's a small chance your Parole Review date will change after this check. This generally only occurs if your sentence is greater than 7 years. This is *not* the date the Parole Board hears your case.

*Parole Review Date.* This is when the Parole Board is scheduled to review your case for possible early release. The Board looks at your programs, conduct, and release plan, and can grant parole, defer your case, or deny parole. If you are granted parole, you will be assigned a Parole Date. If you are deferred, you will be assigned a new Parole Review Date.

### Release dates

*Good Time Release Date.* This is when you'll be released if you keep all your Good Time by avoiding write-ups and doing your assigned programs. If the Parole Board grants parole, you may be released before this date.

*85% Date.* Some serious offenses require at least 85% of a sentence to be served in a facility. If relevant, your Good Time Release Date can’t move earlier than this date.

*Maximum Release Date.* This is the latest date you can be held. You will not serve past this date.


## How does Good Time affect my dates?

In North Dakota, “Good Time” is sentence-reduction credit. It’s counted from day one. Here’s how it works:

- **You get 5 days off for each month of your sentence.** That’s about 60 days off for every year.
- **Counted from day one.** Your Good Time Release Date already includes all your Good Time. It's projected from day one — you start with the full amount applied to your release date, and your job is to keep it
- **What it depends on.** You keep your Good Time by avoiding disciplinary write-ups and doing the programs and work assignments your case manager (CM) recommends. You only need to do the programs in your plan.
- **If you lose Good Time.** Disciplinary write-ups can take Good Time away. When that happens, your Good Time Release Date moves later. The amount depends on how serious the write-up is. Your case manager can explain how a specific write-up would affect you.
- **Parole revocations.** If you’re on parole and your parole is revoked (taken away), the Parole Board can also take away earned Good Time.

## What other credits do I have?

Beyond standard Good Time, other credits can shorten your sentence:

### Jail Time Credit

You receive credit toward your sentence for every day you spent in jail before being moved to DOC custody.

### Jail Good Time

You may receive extra credit for good behavior while you were in jail. This is usually 1 day of credit for every 6 days served.

### Meritorious Credit

Meritorious credit is very rare. It’s only given for things like saving a life or other acts of heroism. The warden gives this credit, and the DOCR director must approve it. Finishing a GED or treatment does not earn meritorious credit — but it does help your parole case.

## Why can’t some dates move earlier?

You may notice that even if you earn extra credits, your release date stays the same. This is usually because of rules that set the earliest release date:

### The 85% Rule

If you were convicted of certain violent offenses, state law requires you to serve at least 85% of your total sentence before you can be released. You still earn Good Time during an 85% sentence — but your release date is whichever is later, your 85% Date or your Good Time Release Date. You can be held past your 85% date if you lose Good Time.

The 85% rule does not apply to sentences imposed when probation is revoked.

*Does it apply to me?* It depends on your specific offense. Your case manager can confirm.

### Minimum Mandatory

Some sentences include a minimum mandatory term — a specific amount of time you must serve before you can be considered for parole. The judge or state law sets this amount.

For most minimum mandatory sentences, credits like Good Time don't move the date you become parole-eligible. You generally keep earning Good Time during the mandatory portion — but that Good Time can't release you (or make you parole-eligible) before the mandatory term is served.

Some specific situations work differently:

- **Certain life sentences with parole.** For Class AA felony life-with-parole, Good Time can reduce the time before you're parole-eligible.
- **DUI and drug minimum mandatories.** DOCR may place you in a recidivism reduction track (DRRT, PRRT, or PARA) that can lead to parole consideration. Ask your case manager if you might qualify.

Your case manager can confirm what type of minimum mandatory you have and how Good Time applies to your specific sentence.

## How does Transitional Housing work?

The Adult Transition Program (ATP) is the final stage of your sentence. You live and work in the community while still in DOCR custody. ATP includes work release (paid employment) and education release (college). Men go through MTP (the men’s program); women go through FTP (the women’s program).

**Important:** ATP doesn’t change your release date. It changes where you live and what you do during the last stretch of your sentence.

### When am I eligible?

- Work release: 8 months or less remaining, housed at MRCC for at least 30 days
- Education release: 12 months or less remaining
- For both: a clean disciplinary record (generally no Level II or III write-ups in the last 6 months), keeping up with recommended programming and treatment, no active warrants or detainers (legal holds from another court or agency), and not having previously turned down parole

If you’re under the 85% rule or a Minimum Mandatory, your ATP eligibility is more limited — usually a maximum of 6 months of work release.

### When will my case manager bring this up?

Case managers usually start ATP conversations about 6 months before your projected release. If you think you’re in that range and haven’t heard from your case manager, submit a kite. Don’t wait to be asked.

Eligible isn’t the same as approved. Meeting the criteria above makes you a candidate. It doesn’t guarantee placement. Decisions weigh your behavior, treatment progress, programming completion, and the specifics of your case. Other residents’ timelines don’t affect yours — staff don’t release in order of who’s been here longest.

### What I should be doing now

- Keep your behavior clean. Disciplinary write-ups in the 6 months before application are the most common reason people get denied.
- Keep up with treatment and programming. If treatment is part of your plan, your core programming generally must be complete before placement.
- Get your documents started early. You’ll need a Social Security card and a government photo ID to work legally. If you don’t have both, you may need to order a birth certificate first, which takes time and money. Ask your case manager to help you start this process as soon as possible — earlier than you think.
- Resolve any holds, warrants, or unpaid restitution. These can block placement even if everything else looks good.

### What can take ATP away after I’m approved?

Approval isn’t final until you actually transfer. Disciplinary issues, missed programming, or other changes can cause approval to be taken away or delayed. Being terminated from ATP after placement returns you to a DOCR facility — and counts against future parole consideration.

### Talk to your case manager about

- Your specific eligibility window (especially if you have multiple sentences, are under 85% / Minimum Mandatory, or are a STEP candidate)
- Where you’d be placed (Centre, Inc., Bismarck Transition Center, or another facility)
- What programming or treatment you still need to complete
- Why an application was denied or delayed, and what you’d need to change to reapply

## How does the STEP Program work?

### What is STEP?

STEP is a sentence-reduction option set by the judge at sentencing. It only applies to certain drug and alcohol cases. If your judgment includes STEP eligibility and you complete the required treatment, you may be released early on parole.

#### Important things to know

- STEP only applies if the judge specified it. Your case manager can confirm.
- If you have another offense that isn’t STEP-eligible, you can’t be released under STEP — even if one of your cases qualifies.
- You can’t add STEP eligibility through good behavior. It’s set by the court.
- Treatment staff usually notify your case manager only a week or two before your release plan needs to be in place.

**What you can do:** Keep up with treatment and ask your case manager whether STEP applies to your case.

## What do I have to do after I’m released?

Your sentence doesn’t always end when you walk out the door. Many residents will have supervision after release.

### Parole Supervision

If you’re granted parole, you’ll be on parole supervision until your Good Time Release Date. You must follow the conditions set by the Parole Board.

### Probation Supervision

Probation is supervision in the community. If your sentence includes probation, it generally starts after parole is complete.

For some offenses — especially felony sex offenses — probation is longer. It can range from 5 to 10 years, or even lifetime for the most serious offenses. The length of this probation is set by state law.

### Registration Requirements

Certain offenses (especially sex offenses) may require you to register with law enforcement after release.

*Planning ahead:* ask your case manager what supervision will apply to you so you can prepare.

## What happens if…

Here are answers to common questions about how different situations affect your dates:

### I lose Good Time

Your Good Time Release Date will move later. The amount depends on how much Good Time was taken. This change will appear on your profile after the disciplinary process is finished and Legal Records updates your record.

### My parole hearing doesn’t result in release

If parole is denied, the Board will tell you what happens next. You may have to serve the rest of your sentence without further parole consideration, get a new parole review date, or be given a condition to satisfy before another review.

### I complete a program

Completing programs is one of the strongest things you can do to strengthen your parole application. It shows the Board you’re preparing for successful reentry. Most programs don’t automatically change your Good Time Release Date — but they can improve your chances of being granted parole earlier.

### I get a new charge while inside

A new conviction will result in a new sentence. In North Dakota, new sentences often run concurrently with your existing sentence. That means a new conviction will usually only change your dates if the new sentence is longer than the time remaining on your sentence. But this isn’t always true: a judge can order a new sentence to run consecutively, which would extend your dates. Contact Legal Records for a new calculation.

### I violate parole and come back

If you're returned to custody for a parole violation, the Parole Board decides how much of your remaining time you'll serve — it could be all of it or part. Legal Records will recalculate your dates after the Board's decision. If you are returning to the custody of the DOCR as a result of parole revocation, you may not receive further parole consideration.

## Who calculates these dates?

The Legal Records Division at the North Dakota Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation calculates these dates. They use your court paperwork and apply North Dakota state law.

## How time is counted

Your sentence is calculated in total days, not calendar months or years. To keep things accurate even during leap years, North Dakota uses these standards: 1 Year = 365.25 days, 1 Month = 30.44 days. Legal Records converts the total time the judge gave you into a single “bucket” of days, then subtracts your credits to find your release dates.

### Updates

Your dates are pulled into this app from the DOCR system every night. Reasons your dates might change include:

- Something has changed on your record (like a disciplinary action or program completion).
- You have had an Initial Review and your Parole Review Date has been set or updated
- You have had a Parole Review and have been granted parole, deferred, or denied

### If something looks wrong

If your dates here don’t match paperwork you’ve received — or you believe there’s an error — submit an Inmate Interview Request (IIR) to the Legal Records Office at your facility.

If you believe you should have received credit for time served that isn’t on your court paperwork, this needs to be fixed by the court. DOCR cannot add it.

## What do the labels on my Parole Board Review Date mean? 

Your case plan shows your Parole Board Review Date with a short label next to the date — like INITIAL, MISC, or INELIGIBLE. This label tells you what kind of review the date is for. There are two kinds of labels.

**Review-type labels** describe what the review actually is:

- **INITIAL** — A file-only review where Parole Board and DOCR staff look at your record to set your Parole Review Date. This applies to residents with more than 3 years left to serve. 
- **INELIGIBLE** — You don't have a regular Parole Board review scheduled. This usually means your release pathway is something other than parole — for example, STEP, an 85% sentence, a life sentence without parole, or certain minimum mandatories. The date you see may be from when a review was originally scheduled, before your eligibility was updated.

**Docket-type labels** describe which group of cases your Parole Review is grouped into. These are all Parole Board Reviews.

- **MISC** — The regular monthly docket. Most residents with a scheduled hearing are on this docket.
- **VIOLATOR** — The docket for residents who returned to prison after a parole violation, or whose parole date is being reconsidered by the Board.
- **PERSONAL** — A hearing where you'll appear in person, rather than by file review. The Board schedules these for specific cases.

If you're not sure what kind of date you're looking at, ask your case manager.

## What if I have more than one sentence?

Many residents are serving more than one sentence at a time. How those sentences fit together affects your dates.

### Concurrent

Concurrent means the sentences run at the same time. You serve them together. The longer one usually decides when you're released.

### Consecutive

Consecutive means the sentences run back-to-back. You finish one, then start the next. This makes your total time longer.

The judge decides whether your sentences are concurrent or consecutive. It's written in your court paperwork.

**What you see on this app:** The dates shown here are a summary across all your sentences combined — not the dates for any one sentence on its own. If you want to see how each sentence is calculated separately, refer to your time computation sheet.

## Questions about your dates?

Every sentence is unique. Your dates are based on your specific crime, the judgment from the court, and your behavior while in custody.

If you believe there is an error in your calculation, you may submit an Inmate Interview Request (IIR) to the Legal Records Office at your facility.
